journal-title-group.journal-title: Advances in experimental medicine and biology

title-group.article-title: The Structural Adaptations that Mediate Mechanical Load-Induced Changes in Muscle Mass

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Comparative Biosciences, University of Wisconsin - Madison, Madison, WI, USA
2School of Veterinary Medicine, University of Wisconsin - Madison, Madison, WI, USA

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding Author (Lead Contact): Troy A. Hornberger, 2015 Linden Drive, Madison WI, 53706, troy.hornberger@wisc.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

abstract.p: During the last century, it has become clear that mechanical signals play a pivotal role in the regulation of skeletal muscle mass. For instance, numerous studies have shown that an increase in mechanical loading will lead to an increase in muscle mass, and a decrease in mechanical loading will lead to a decrease in muscle mass. These same studies have not only led to the widely adopted terms of “mechanical load-induced growth” and “disuse atrophy”, but they have also shaped our understanding of the macroscopic, microscopic, and ultrastructural adaptations that contribute to the changes in muscle mass. These structural adaptations serve as the foundational events via which mechanical signals regulate skeletal muscle mass and, in this chapter, we will explore what is known about these adaptations and pinpoint the critical gaps in knowledge that remain to be filled.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Skeletal muscle is the largest organ in the human body and contributes to approximately 35% of an individual’s body mass (100). Skeletal muscles are the motors that drive basic movements and they regulate various physiological functions, including the regulation of body temperature, metabolism, and breathing. They also act as endocrine organs through the secretion of myokines (51, 156, 167, 184). Given these roles, it’s not surprising that the maintenance of muscle mass is closely linked to overall health and quality of life. For instance, low muscle mass has been associated with an elevated risk of conditions such as type II diabetes, heart disease, and increased mortality (3, 172, 176, 191, 212, 215), and the importance of preserving muscle mass becomes particularly pronounced with aging, as the age-related decline in muscle mass has strong correlations with disability, loss of independence, mortality, and substantial healthcare costs (estimated at $20-40 billion annually in the United States alone) (72, 136, 143, 165). Hence, the development of therapies that can sustain, restore, or enhance muscle mass is of immense clinical and fiscal significance. However, the development of such therapies will require a comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms governing the size of this essential organ.

It has been well established that skeletal muscle mass can be regulated by a variety of different stimuli, with one of the most potent stimuli being mechanical signals (4, 28, 154). Indeed, as far back as the 5th century BC, Hippocrates understood the principle that “exercise strengthens and inactivity wastes” (178). Hence, it would be fair to argue that the notion that changes in mechanical signals/loading regulate skeletal muscle mass has been recognized for at least 2,500 years (187). Over the last century, a plethora of human- and animal-based studies have provided further support for this concept and led to the widely adopted terms of “mechanical load-induced growth” and “disuse atrophy”, respectively (102, 163). Such studies have also shaped our understanding of the macroscopic, microscopic, and ultrastructural changes that contribute to the changes in muscle mass. In this chapter, we will review what is known about these structural adaptations along with the major gaps in knowledge that still exist.

sec.title: Overview of Skeletal Muscle Structure
sec.p: To appreciate the structural adaptations that mediate mechanically induced changes in muscle mass the reader first needs to appreciate the overall structural organization of skeletal muscle. As we have previously described, we believe that one of the easiest ways to appreciate this organization is to view skeletal muscle as a hierarchy of contractile machinery that is visible at the macroscopic level (viewable without magnification), followed by the microscopic level (viewable with standard microscopy), and finally the ultrastructural level (viewable with high-resolution microscopy). Below we will summarize the key elements that are found at each of these levels. For alternative illustrations and more comprehensive discussions on this topic, the reader is referred to the following reviews (66, 89, 111, 133).

At the macroscopic level, skeletal muscles establish connections with bones through tendinous attachments and enact their contractile function by facilitating movement and articulation of the skeletal system. As depicted in Figure 1, skeletal muscles are encased by an outer layer of connective tissue called the epimysium. Beneath the epimysium, there are bundles of muscle fibers which are referred to as fascicles, and the fascicles are surrounded by another layer of connective tissue called the perimysium (66). Notably, in the majority of skeletal muscles, the fascicles and their corresponding muscle fibers do not align directly with the longitudinal axis of the muscle but instead are positioned at an angle known as the pennation angle (Fig. 2).

At the microscopic level, a cross-section of skeletal muscle will reveal the presence of individual muscle fibers (Fig. 1A–B). The muscle fibers are multinucleated cells that are surrounded by a membrane called the sarcolemma and an outer layer of connective tissue called the endomysium. The interstitial space between the muscle fibers houses various cell types including fibroblasts, immune cells, pericytes, and fibro-adipogenic progenitors (Fig. 1A–C) (25, 179). Nestled between the endomysium and the sarcolemma is another class of cells called satellite cells which function as muscle stem cells and are critically important for myogenesis (Fig. 1A) (129, 131, 179). Finally, everything that resides beneath the sarcolemma of the muscle fibers is collectively referred to as the sarcoplasm and it contains the contractile machinery that is essential for the function of the muscle fibers.

At the ultrastructural level, it has been demonstrated that ~80% of each muscle fiber is occupied by branched rod-like structures called myofibrils (77, 115, 117, 166, 188, 210). The myofibrils are composed of a long in-series array of force-generating elements called sarcomeres and are encased by a mesh-like membrane called the sarcoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria (Fig. 1A, D, and E). The sarcomeres within the myofibrils carry out their function via the active sliding and interaction of the thick and thin myofilaments (97, 98), and in a longitudinal view, it can be seen that the sarcomeres consist of regions called the Z-disc, the I-band which contains the thin (actin) myofilaments, and the A-band which contains the thick (myosin) myofilaments (26, 84). Notably, the length of sarcomeres is highly conserved between species and typically ranges from 2.0 to 2.5 μm in length (31, 84). This dimension is determined by the distance between the two Z-discs of the sarcomere and alterations in this length can profoundly influence force production by the sarcomeres (31, 50, 84).

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Changes in Muscle Mass – The Macroscopic Level
Whole Muscle
sec.p: Like all materials, the mass of skeletal muscle is a function of its volume and density, and we are not aware of any studies that have reported substantive mechanical load-induced changes in density. As such, it can be inferred that mechanical load-induced changes in mass are mediated by alterations in the volume of the muscle. In skeletal muscle, volume is determined by the length and cross-sectional area (CSA) of the muscle, and thus, mechanical load-induced changes in mass can be mediated by alterations in the length and/or the cross-sectional area (CSA) of the muscle. In the following sections, we will consider studies that have examined whether these types of alterations occur at the whole muscle level.

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Growth
sec.p: An increase in mass that results from an increase in length is referred to as longitudinal growth and it can occur in response to a variety of different perturbations. For instance, during development, muscles can undergo a greater than twofold increase in length from the time of birth to the cessation of bone growth (17, 74, 207). In adults, longitudinal growth can be induced by placing muscles in a chronically stretched state (217). For instance, in rats, it has been reported that immobilization of the lower hindlimb in a fully dorsiflexed position can result in a more than 20% increase in the length of the soleus muscle (12). Surgical procedures for limb lengthening can also result in a greater than 20% increase in muscle length (33, 171). Furthermore, extreme models of chronic mechanical overload such as the surgical removal of the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles (i.e., synergist ablation) have been reported to lead to an increase in the length of the plantaris muscle (158, 180). Hence, it is evident that both adolescent and adult skeletal muscles are capable of undergoing longitudinal growth.

Although skeletal muscles are capable of longitudinal growth, most perturbations that lead to an increase in mechanical loading do not result in a significant change in the length of the muscle (94, 104). Instead, growth resulting from an increase in mechanical loading typically manifests as an increase in the CSA of the muscle (i.e., radial growth). For example, in humans, participating in 8-16 weeks of a resistance exercise program will typically lead to a 5-30% increase in whole muscle CSA, but no notable changes in muscle length (1, 9, 18, 43, 96, 104, 120, 155, 201). Interestingly, the reported increase in CSA is often greater than the observed increase in muscle volume/mass. A notable example of this paradox is illustrated by Roman et al. (1993), who reported a 14% increase in the volume of the elbow flexors after 12 weeks of resistance exercise, yet the CSA of the muscle at the mid-belly increased by 23% (155). However, the extent of the increase in CSA became progressively smaller towards the proximal and distal ends of the muscle. In other words, the study by Roman et al. (1993) revealed that the radial growth response was not evenly distributed along the length of the muscle. Indeed, regional variations in the magnitude of radial growth have been documented in numerous animal and human-based studies, and from our perspective, this is an often overlooked aspect of the mechanical load-induced growth response (6, 11, 27, 61, 70, 96, 104, 134, 159).

sec.title: Disuse Atrophy
sec.p: At the whole muscle level, the structural adaptations that mediate disuse atrophy are effectively the opposite of the mechanisms that mediate mechanical load-induced growth. Disuse-induced atrophy, for instance, can be driven by a reduction in the length and/or the CSA of the muscle. The term “longitudinal atrophy” is used when the loss of muscle mass stems from a decrease in the length of the muscle whereas the term “radial atrophy” is used when the decrease in muscle mass results from a decrease in CSA. As outlined in Section 2, muscles are attached to the bones via tendons, and in adults, the length of the bones remains constant (8, 17, 20). Consequently, the resting length of muscles is commonly perceived as being invariable, and as a result, it has rarely been measured in studies of disuse atrophy. Nevertheless, studies that have reported measurements of muscle length have consistently shown that when a muscle is chronically confined to a shortened position (e.g., cast immobilization) it can undergo significant longitudinal atrophy (41, 85, 175, 211). For example, in rats, Coutinho et al., (2004) observed a 19% reduction in the length of the solues muscle after 3 weeks of immobilization in a shortened position (41). Conversely, Spector et al. (1982) found that immobilizing the rat hindlimb in a neutral position for 4 weeks led to a substantial 41-49% decrease in the mass of muscles such as the soleus, medial gastrocnemius, and tibialis anterior, yet none of these muscles exhibited a significant alteration in length (175). Notably, in the same study, additional experiments were conducted with the muscles immobilized in a shortened position, and in these experiments, all of the muscles experienced a significant 12-21% decrease in length (175). Hence, it seems that when disuse is coupled with a chronic shortening of the muscle it will lead to longitudinal atrophy but this does not occur when the muscles are subjected to disuse in a chronically neutral position.

The research conducted by Spector et al. (1982) revealed that a very large disuse-induced decrease in muscle mass can occur in the absence of longitudinal atrophy (175). Therefore, the declines in muscle mass observed in their study were likely mediated by a decrease in the whole muscle CSA / radial atrophy (175). Indeed, radial atrophy at the whole muscle level has been documented in various models of disuse atrophy including, space flight (160, 181), bed rest (22, 24, 48, 57, 64, 113, 122, 126–128, 173), unilateral lower limb suspension (ULLS) (2, 45, 87), and immobilization (91, 106, 144, 175, 182, 194, 196, 214). Notably, in the majority of these studies, measurements of the whole muscle CSA were taken at the mid-belly of the muscle (i.e., at 50% of the muscle length) (45, 127, 181). However, just like the radial growth of muscles that occurs in response to an increase in mechanical loading, the radial atrophy that occurs in response to disuse is not always homogenous along the length of the muscle (64, 113, 122, 173).

sec.title: Muscle Fascicles
sec.p: As a guiding principle, it is beneficial to bear in mind that mechanical load changes that occur at one level of muscle structure will be mediated by changes that occur at the preceding level of the structural hierarchy (e.g., myofibrils → muscle fibers → fascicles → whole muscle). Thus, according to this guideline, the longitudinal and radial growth/atrophy that occurs at the whole muscle level will be mediated by alterations that occur at the level of the fascicles. Importantly, when contemplating how alterations at the level of the fascicles could induce changes at the whole muscle level, it is essential to consider the architectural properties of the muscle. The importance of this cannot be understated because it would be counterintuitive to propose that robust longitudinal atrophy at the whole muscle level could exclusively be mediated by radial atrophy of the fascicles, or that robust radial growth at the whole muscle level could be exclusively mediated by longitudinal growth of the fascicles. However, as illustrated in Figure 3, these are both realistic possibilities. Readers who are not familiar with the geometric principles shown in Figure 3 are encouraged to view the work of Maxwell et al. (1974) because further consideration of this work will help the reader appreciate that changes in whole muscle CSA and/or length can be mediated by changes in fascicle length and/or diameter and that the exact mechanism(s) that drive the adaptations at the whole muscle level will depend on the inherent architectural properties of the muscle as well as the physical constraints under which the adaptations occur. For additional discussions and context-specific examples of this topic please refer to (101, 102, 119, 163).

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Growth
sec.p: Having addressed how alterations in fascicle length and fascicle diameter can induce changes at the whole muscle level, we will now consider the studies that have examined whether an increase in mechanical loading alters these properties. Specifically, we will first consider whether an increase in mechanical loading leads to changes in fascicle length, and fortunately, this topic has been addressed in numerous studies (5, 9, 16, 52, 54, 61, 104, 120, 146, 170, 185, 193). For example, a review by Ema et al. (2016) identified 25 different studies which have shown that resistance exercise can lead to a significant increase in fascicle length with the mean increase in these studies being 12.4%. The review by Ema et al. (2016) also demonstrated that the resistance exercise-induced increase in whole muscle size and fascicle length is highly correlated (53). Thus, there is strong support for the notion that an increase in mechanical loading can induce longitudinal growth of the fascicles, and this effect can, at least in part, drive the increase in CSA that occurs at the whole muscle level.

In contrast to the large number of studies that have examined how an increase in mechanical loading can influence fascicle length, very few, if any, have directly examined how an increase in mechanical loading can influence fascicle diameter. However, as detailed by Maxwell et al. (1974), and as shown in Figure 3, a direct relationship exists between fascicle diameter and the pennation angle of the fascicles. Specifically, Figure 3D illustrates that when the length of the muscle, and the number of fascicles are held constant, then an increase in fascicle diameter will lead to an increase in the pennation angle of the fascicles (Fig. 3D). No studies that we are aware of have ever shown that changes in mechanical loading can lead to an alteration in the number of fascicles, and since most forms of increased mechanical loading do not alter the length of the muscle (e.g., resistance exercise), it can be inferred that an than increase in pennation angle of the fascicles would be indicative of an increase in the diameter of the fascicles. Importantly, Ema et al. (2016) identified 46 different studies which have shown that resistance exercise can lead to a significant increase in the pennation angle of the fascicles with the mean increase in these studies being 13.5%. Thus, just like changes in fascicle length, there is substantial support for the notion that an increase in mechanical loading can lead to an increase in fascicle diameter/pennation angle. Indeed, a comprehensive analysis of the existing literature suggests that mechanical load-induced growth at the whole muscle level can be mediated by both longitudinal and radial growth of fascicles, and the exact contribution of these adaptations will be determined by factors such as the type of mechanical loads that are placed on the muscle (e.g., concentric vs. eccentric contractions) and the inherent architectural properties of the muscle being studied (e.g., fusiform, unipennate, bipennate, etc.) (53, 62, 63, 186).

sec.title: Disuse Atrophy
sec.p: Having discussed how a mechanical load-induced increase in fascicle length and diameter/pennation angle can induce changes at the whole muscle level, we will now explore whether disuse alters these properties of the fascicles. Fortunately, this topic has been readily explored. For instance, numerous studies in humans have shown that various forms of disuse including ULLS, bed rest, and space flight lead to a decrease in fascicle length (34, 45, 46, 64, 109, 132, 145, 162, 168). Notably, while it seems that longitudinal atrophy at the whole muscle level only occurs when disuse coincides with a chronic shortening of the muscle, longitudinal atrophy of the fascicles can be observed when muscles are subjected to disuse in a predominantly neutral position (34, 45, 168). For example, with ULLS the knee and ankle are maintained in a neutral position, and De Boer et al. (2007) have shown that after 23 days of ULLS, the length of fascicles in the vastus lateralis muscle decreased by 8% (45). Similarly, Campbell, et al. (2013) studied the length of the fascicles in all four heads of the quadriceps and found a 6-11% decrease in fascicle length after 23 days of ULLS (34). Several studies have also shown that bed rest leads to longitudinal atrophy of the fascicles (46, 64, 145, 162). Thus, it appears that disuse can lead to longitudinal atrophy of the fascicles and this effect is not dependent on chronic shortening of the muscle during the period of disuse. Moreover, according to Maxwell’s model, longitudinal atrophy of the fascicles is likely, at least in part, responsible for the disuse-induced decrease in CSA that occurs at the whole muscle level.

As detailed by Sayed et al. (2023), a decrease in fascicle diameter could also lead to a decrease in whole muscle length and/or CSA (163). However, to the best of our knowledge, no reports have directly addressed whether disuse alters fascicle diameter. On the other hand, numerous studies have examined the impact of disuse on the pennation angle of the fascicles. For instance, it has been shown that the disuse that occurs in response to ULLS can lead to a decrease in the pennation angle of the fascicles (34, 45, 168), and the same point is true when bedrest is used as the model of disuse (46, 145). Indeed, a decrease in the pennation angle of the fascicles in various plantar flexor muscles has even been observed after prolonged space flight (109). However, during space flight, humans assume a plantarflexed foot drop posture, and in this position, the plantarflexors are maintained in a shortened position (37, 183). This is noteworthy because the chronically shortened position of the plantarflexors would likely lead to a decrease in whole muscle length, and according to Maxwell’s model, if the length of the muscle decreased, then a decrease in the pennation angle of the fascicles would underrepresent the true decrease in fascicle diameter that occurred (119). Thus, although no studies have directly addressed whether disuse leads to the radial atrophy of fascicles, reasonable deductions can be made and these deductions consistently suggest that disuse leads to the radial atrophy of fascicles. For additional details on this topic, the reader is referred to Sayed et al. (2023) (163).

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Changes in Muscle Mass – The Microscopic Level
sec.p: As mentioned above, changes that occur at one level of muscle structure will be mediated by changes that occur at the preceding level of the structural hierarchy. In the previous section, we described how changes in mechanical loading can alter the length and diameter of the fascicles, and we will now examine how these alterations are mediated by changes that occur at the microscopic level (i.e., at the level of the muscle fibers).

When contemplating how changes in mechanical loading could induce changes in fascicle length, it’s important to bear in mind that fascicles are comprised of bundles of muscle fibers, and typically, the muscle fibers run the entire length of the fascicles (140, 141). For such fascicles, changes in length would most likely be mediated by an increase/decrease in the length of the individual fibers. However, in some muscles, fascicles are constructed from muscle fibers that only run a portion of the fascicle length. In these types of fascicles, the fibers exhibit intrafascicular terminations that interdigitate with the ends of other fibers within the fascicle (69, 90, 114, 148). In theory, changes in the length of these types of fascicles could be mediated by an increase/decrease in the length of the individual muscle fibers and/or an increase/decrease in the number of muscle fibers in-series. However, we are not aware of any studies that have examined whether changes in mechanical loading can affect the number of in-series muscle fibers. Thus, in this section, it will be assumed the changes in the length of fascicles are primarily mediated by changes in the length of their associated muscle fibers.

As stated earlier, changes in mechanical loading can also lead to alterations in the diameter of the fascicles. Importantly, the diameter of the fascicles is largely dictated by: i) the number of muscle fibers per cross-section, and ii) the CSA of the muscle fibers within the cross-section. Thus, changes in either of the variables could lead to changes in fascicle diameter. With regard to the number of fibers per cross-section, changes could be induced through a net increase (hyperplasia) or decrease (hypoplasia) in the number of muscle fibers. However, as discussed in our previous reviews (102, 163), the notion that changes in mechanical loading can lead to hyperplasia and/or hypoplasia is controversial, and we have concluded that physiologically relevant changes in mechanical loading (e.g., resistance exercise, bed rest, etc.) generally do not lead to a substantive change in the number of muscle fibers. As such, the concepts of hyperplasia and hypoplasia will not be further elaborated upon in this chapter. Nevertheless, several studies have reported that changes in mechanical loading can lead to changes in the number of fibers per cross-section (29, 82, 83, 216), and although it is not intuitively obvious, we believe that these effects can largely be explained by changes in muscle fiber length and/or muscle fiber diameter. The logic behind our position has been detailed in some of our previous manuscripts, and readers who are not familiar with this topic are strongly encouraged to review the text associated with Figure 2 in Jorgenson et al. (2019) (101) and Figures 3 and 4 in Sayed et al. (2023) (163).

Given the points raised above, the microscopic changes that drive mechanical load-induced changes in fascicle length and fascicle diameter are most likely the result of changes in the CSA and/or length of the muscle fibers. Thus, the following sections will summarize the results of studies that have examined how changes in mechanical loading affect these microscopic properties.

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Growth
sec.p: To date, numerous studies have shown that an increase in mechanical loading can lead to an increase in the length of muscle fibers (6, 7, 112, 157, 158). As a case in point, Roy et al. (1982) revealed that chronic mechanical loading of the plantaris muscle via synergist ablation led to a 10% increase in the length of the fibers (157). In another example, Alway et al. (1989) attached a weight to the wings of quails and, after 30 days, it was determined that the length of the fibers in the anterior latissimus dorsi muscle had increased by 24% (6). Although numerous studies have shown that extreme forms of increased mechanical loading can induce longitudinal growth of the muscle fibers, this topic has not been readily addressed with more physiologically relevant models. For instance, it has been shown that downhill walking (32), downhill running (116), and electrically evoked contractions (92), can all lead to an increase in the number of in-series sarcomeres: however, none of these studies reported measurements of muscle fiber length. Indeed, to the best of our knowledge, only one study has reported measurements of muscle fiber length within the context of a physiologically relevant model (94). Specifically, in this study, rats were subjected to 8 weeks of a form of resistance exercise called ladder climbing, and the outcomes revealed that the exercise led to a 24% increase in the physiological CSA area of the flexor hallucis longus muscle, but the length of the fibers was not significantly altered. Importantly, however, the study did not address whether the increase in the CSA of the muscle was mediated by longitudinal and/or radial growth of the fascicles, and thus, it is difficult to extrapolate a strong conclusion from this single observation. As such, we believe that it would be appropriate to conclude that an extreme increase in mechanical loading can induce longitudinal growth of the muscle fibers, but whether this type of growth occurs in response to physiologically relevant forms of increased mechanical loading remains to be determined.

In contrast to the limited research on longitudinal growth, a vast number of studies have examined how a physiologically relevant increase in mechanical loading can impact the CSA of muscle fibers. Indeed, radial growth of the muscle fibers (i.e., an increase in muscle fiber CSA, also often referred to as muscle fiber hypertrophy) is, by far, the most widely recognized contributor to the mechanical load-induced growth of skeletal muscle. This is not surprising when one considers that the ability of an increase in mechanical loading to induce radial growth of the muscle fibers has been known for over 100 years (124). Moreover, it has consistently been observed in humans, dogs, cats, mice, rats, hamsters, and birds (73, 77, 81, 124, 155, 164, 174). Importantly, with some of the animal-based studies, extreme forms of increased mechanical loading were employed (e.g., synergist ablation, wing-weighting, etc.), whereas others utilized interventions that were intended to mimic human resistance exercise. For instance, we recently trained mice to perform a full-body/multi-joint resistance exercise called weight pulling and then subjected the mice to a training protocol that mimicked a traditional human progressive resistance exercise paradigm. Just like progressive resistance exercise in humans, it was determined that 13 weeks of training led to an increase in the CSA of fibers in various muscles, and that the magnitude of the increase was similar to the 10-35% increase that is typically observed in humans after 8-16 weeks of training (1, 18, 35, 40, 67, 88, 110, 123, 155). Notably, however, this magnitude of increase is relatively small when compared to the outcomes that have been observed with more extreme forms of increased mechanical loading. For instance, Antonio and Gonyea (1993) reported a staggering 142% increase in the CSA of the fibers of the anterior latissimus dorsi muscle after just 16 days of wing-weighting (10). Thus, it can be strongly concluded an increase in mechanical loading can induce radial growth of the muscle fibers, and it appears that the capacity for this type of growth is quite substantial.

sec.title: Disuse Atrophy
sec.p: When examining the topic of disuse-induced changes in fiber length, one can find numerous studies which have shown that subjecting muscles to disuse in a chronically shortened position can lead to a decrease in fiber length (i.e., longitudinal atrophy of the muscle fibers) (41, 169, 175, 178, 199, 206, 211). For instance, Witzmann et al. (1982) observed a 27% decrease in the length of the fibers of the soleus muscle after it had been immobilized in a shortened position for 42 days. Likewise, Spector et al. (1982) found a 26% decrease in the length of the fibers in the medial gastrocnemius muscle after 4 weeks of being immobilized in a shortened position (175).

Although numerous studies have shown that disuse in a chronically shortened position can lead to longitudinal atrophy of the muscle fibers, it is not clear whether the same effect happens when muscles are subjected to disuse in a neutral position. Indeed, we are only aware of one study that has directly investigated this topic. Specifically, Spector et al. (1982) immobilized the lower hindlimb of rats in a neutral position for 4 weeks and concluded that it had no significant effect on the length of the fibers in the soleus, medial gastrocnemius, or tibialis anterior muscles (175). However, it’s important to note that the rats used in this study were quite young (e.g., estimated to be 6-7 weeks of age according to the reported body weights) (39). This is a noteworthy point because the longitudinal growth of bones in rats can continue until approximately 20 weeks of age (95). Thus, it is likely that the immobilized muscles that were studied by Spector et al. (1982) were still experiencing a chronic lengthening stimulus from the longitudinal growth of the bones, and thus, caution should be exercised when interpreting the results from this study. Building on this point, it’s worthwhile to revisit the studies discussed in Section 3.2.2 which demonstrated that longitudinal atrophy of the fascicles can be observed when muscles are subjected to disuse in a predominantly neutral position (34, 45, 168). Specifically, these studies demonstrated that a decrease in fascicle length could be observed in various muscles including all four heads of the quadriceps as well as the lateral head of the gastrocnemius. Existing evidence suggests that, in all of these muscles, the fibers span the entire length of the fascicles (65, 200, 202). Hence, it would seem reasonable to infer that the disuse-induced decrease in fascicle length was mediated by longitudinal atrophy of the fibers. However, this inference is purely theoretical, and as such, the only prudent conclusion that can be drawn is that additional research is needed to establish whether disuse in a neutral position leads to longitudinal atrophy of the muscle fibers.

Unlike the limited number of studies that have examined the impact of disuse on fiber length, a plethora of studies have examined the impact that disuse has on the CSA of muscle fibers. These studies have utilized various models including hindlimb unloading (47, 68, 86, 125, 130, 206), bed rest (13, 21, 213), space flight (19, 49, 58, 177, 204), immobilization (41, 79, 80, 135, 196), and ULLS (30, 34, 87), and consistently reported that disuse leads to a decrease in the CSA of the muscle fibers. For instance, Sung et al. (2013) reported that 13 days of space flight led to a 39% decrease in the CSA of mouse gastrocnemius muscle fibers (177). Likewise, Arentson-Lantz et al. (2016) found that 14 days of bed rest led to a 24% decrease in the CSA of human vastus lateralis muscle fibers. Thus, when considering the large number of studies, and highly consistent findings, it can be strongly concluded that disuse leads to radial atrophy of the muscle fibers.

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Changes in Muscle Mass – The Ultrastructural Level
sec.p: Up to this stage it has been concluded that, in general, an increase in mechanical loading will induce the longitudinal and/or radial growth of the muscle fibers, whereas disuse will induce longitudinal and/or radial atrophy of the muscle fibers. According to our guiding principle, these changes will be mediated by the alterations that occur at the preceding level within the hierarchy of the structural elements, which, in this case, refers to the ultrastructure of the muscle. Specifically, ~80% of the muscle fiber volume is composed of myofibrils, and therefore, it can be reasoned that longitudinal and/or radial growth/atrophy of the muscle fibers will be largely mediated by changes that occur at the level of the myofibrils (117, 166). Accordingly, in this section, we will explore how changes in mechanical loading can induce growth/atrophy of the myofibrils.

sec.title: Alterations in the Number of In-Series Sarcomeres During the Longitudinal Growth/Atrophy of Muscle Fibers
sec.p: Muscle fibers are filled with myofibrils, and the myofibrils are composed of a long in-series array of sarcomeres. Thus, it can be inferred that an increase/decrease in the length of the muscle fibers would be mediated by alterations in either the length of the sarcomeres and/or the in-series addition/elimination of sarcomeres. When considering these possibilities, it is important to recognize that the optimal length of sarcomeres is highly conserved (~2.5 μm), and most muscles operate within a narrow range (94 ± 13%) of the sarcomere’s optimal length (31, 84).In other words, it appears that significant emphasis is placed on preserving the optimal length of the sarcomeres, and therefore, the longitudinal growth/atrophy of muscle fibers would most likely be mediated by the in-series addition/elimination of sarcomeres.

sec.title: Mechanical Load-Induced Growth
sec.p: In support of the notion that the longitudinal growth of muscle fibers is mediated by the in-series addition of sarcomeres, Lindsey et al. (2002) used a limb-lengthening procedure to subject the tibialis anterior muscles of goats to chronic stretch and found that it induced a remarkable 47% increase in fiber length, a 42% increase in the number of in-series sarcomeres, but no significant change in the length of the sarcomeres (112). Aoki et al. (2009) also reported that just 4 days of chronic stretch led to a 27% increase in the number of in-series sarcomeres in the soleus muscle fibers of rats (12). Indeed, these, and many other studies (12, 15, 32, 107, 108, 112, 116, 118, 207, 208, 217), have consistently found that an increase in mechanical loading can lead to the in-series addition of sarcomeres, and it appears that this type of growth can transpire in a very rapid manner.

sec.title: Disuse Atrophy
sec.p: As detailed in Section 4.1.2, disuse can lead to the longitudinal atrophy of muscle fibers, and numerous studies have shown that this is associated with the elimination of in-series sarcomeres (41, 79, 105, 169, 178, 199, 209, 211). For example, Tabary et al. (1972) observed a ~34% decrease in fiber length and a ~40% reduction in the number of in-series sarcomeres in the soleus muscles of cats after they had been immobilized in a shortened position for 21-43 days (178). Similarly, Shah et al. (2001) reported a 26% decrease in fiber length and a 26% reduction in the number of in-series sarcomeres at 28 days after mouse soleus muscles had been immobilized in a shortened position. Similar declines in both fiber length and the number of in-series sarcomeres have also been documented with hindlimb suspension (105, 199). Thus, it can be concluded that the disuse-induced longitudinal atrophy of muscle fibers is largely mediated by the elimination of in-series sarcomeres. However, it bears noting that all of the aforementioned studies focused on muscles that were subjected to disuse in a shortened position, and whether the same phenomena occur when muscles are subjected to disuse in a neutral position has, to the best of our knowledge, not been investigated.

sec.title: Alterations in Myofibril CSA and Myofibril Number During the Radial Growth/Atrophy of Muscle Fibers
sec.p: In Section 4 it was concluded that mechanically induced changes in muscle mass are driven, at least in part, by radial growth/atrophy of the muscle fibers. Since the majority of the muscle fiber volume consists of myofibrils, it stands to reason that the radial growth/atrophy will primarily be mediated by changes at the level of the myofibrils. However, alterations in the interstitial elements (e.g., the sarcoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria, intracellular fluid, etc.) could also contribute to the radial growth/atrophy of muscle fibers and, when considering this possibility, it helps to think about specific tension which is defined as the maximal isometric force per unit CSA of the muscle fiber. More precisely, maximal isometric force production is directly dependent on the number of force-generating elements (i.e., sarcomeres/myofibrils) that act in-parallel with the line of force production (38, 59). Thus, if the CSA of the muscle fiber increases and specific tension remains constant, then it can be inferred that the radial growth was mediated by a proportionate addition of both force-generating and non-force-generating elements. Conversely, if the CSA of a muscle fiber increases and specific tension decreases, then it can be inferred that the radial growth was mediated by a disproportionately greater increase in the amount of non-force-generating elements. Under certain circumstances, these inferences might not be correct, but generally speaking, measurements of specific tension will provide important insight into the mechanisms that drove the radial growth/atrophy of the muscle fibers. As such, it’s important to note that most studies have shown that specific tension is fairly well-preserved in muscle fibers that have undergone radial growth/atrophy as a result of a change in mechanical loading (36, 42, 44, 55, 60, 121, 137, 138, 189, 190, 203, 205).

Given that specific tension is fairly well-preserved, and that specific tension is dependent on the number of in-parallel force-generating elements, it would follow that the radial growth/atrophy of muscle fibers is mediated by a propionate change in the CSA that is occupied by the myofibrils and the interstitial elements. Consistent with this expectation, several studies have shown that the radial growth of muscle fibers that occurs in response to an increase in mechanical loading is associated with very minimal changes (≤ 4%) in the relative proportion of the CSA that is occupied by the myofibrils (14, 77, 115, 117, 166). However, we are not aware of any studies that have directly examined this concept with models of disuse. Nonetheless, qualitative observations such as those described by Pellegrino C, and Franzini C. (1963) “transverse sections for electron microscopy fail generally to show that the fibrils depart from the normal in their form and distribution at the 1st week of atrophy” (142, 195), suggest that just like radial growth, the radial atrophy of muscle fibers that occurs in response to disuse is mediated by a proportionate decrease in the area that is occupied by the myofibrils and the interstitial elements. Since the bulk of the muscle fiber volume is composed of myofibrils, it stands to reason that the radial growth/atrophy of muscle fibers will largely be mediated by: (i) a change in the CSA of the individual myofibrils, and/or, (ii) a change in the number of myofibrils per muscle fiber. In the remaining sections, we will summarize the outcomes from studies that have addressed these possibilities.

sec.title: Changes in Myofibril CSA vs. Myofibril Number During Mechanical Load-Induced Growth
sec.p: In 1982, MacDougall and colleagues reported that 6 months of resistance exercise resulted in a 22-25% increase in muscle fiber CSA, whereas the proportion of the CSA that was occupied by the myofibrils was not significantly altered (84 vs. 83% in the pre- and post-trained states, respectively) (117). In other words, the study found that the total CSA of the myofibrils per fiber increased by around 23%. However, whether this effect was due to radial growth of the pre-existing myofibrils and/or an increase in the number of myofibrils per fiber (i.e., myofibrillogenesis) was not determined (117). In fact, we are only aware of three studies that have attempted to address these possibilites.

In one such study, Wang et al. (1993) had a group of women engage in 18 weeks of resistance exercise and found that it led to a significant increase in the CSA of all of the major fiber types in the vastus lateralis muscle (i.e., Type I, IIA, IIAB and IIB) (198). The mean increase in CSA of the major fiber types was 24%, and none of the fiber types showed a significant change in the proportion of the CSA that was occupied by the myofibrils. Moreover, measurements of myofibril CSA revealed that only Type IIB fibers showed a statistically significant alteration (16% decrease). Importantly, measurements of myofibril number were not determined, however, the mean area that was occupied by the myofibrils in each of the fiber types was reported. Thus, by utilizing this data, we were able to make estimates about the number of myofibrils per fiber (i.e., the CSA occupied the myofibrils divided by the myofibril CSA). Our estimates revealed that, on average, the 18 weeks of resistance exercise led to a 29% increase in the number of myofibrils per fiber (18, 21, 54, and 28% increase for Type I, IIA, IIA/B and IIB fibers, respectively). Hence, the data in this study appear to suggest that the radial growth of muscle fibers that occurs after several months of resistance exercise is exclusively due to myofibrillogenesis.

Another study that examined how an increase in mechanical loading impacts the morphological properties of the myofibrils was conducted by Ashmore and Summers (1981). Specifically, these investigators used a spring-loaded device to subject the wings of young chickens to 7 days of chronic stretch (14, 93). Earlier research by the same group had shown that 7 days of chronic stretch led to a ~55% increase in CSA of the fibers in the patigialis muscle (15), and their 1981 publication revealed that this was associated with a 36% increase in the CSA of the individual myofibrils. Unfortunately, the total number of myofibrils per fiber was not determined: however, the 1981 study did report values for the proportion of the fiber CSA that was occupied by myofibrils (67% vs. 71% for control vs. stretched, respectively). Thus, by combining the data from these two studies, we were able to infer that chronic stretch led to a ~64% increase in the total CSA of the myofibrils per fiber. Since the average CSA of the individual myofibrils only increased by 36%, the remaining increase in the total CSA of the myofibrils would presumably have been due to a ~21% increase in the number of myofibrils per fiber. In other words, the work by Ashmore and Summers (1981) seems to indicate that the rapid radial growth of the muscle fibers that occurred in response to 7 days of chronic stretch was due to both radial growth of the myofibrils and myofibrillogenesis. Intriguingly, this study also showed that 7 days of chronic stretch led to a 2.6-fold increase in the proportion of myofibrils that presented with signs of splitting (15). This is intriguing because it aligns with the notion that the putative increase in myofibril number may have been mediated by myofibril splitting as described in the seminal work by Dr. Goldspink (75, 76, 78, 139).

The inferences drawn from the above studies suggest that an increase in mechanical loading induces myofibrillogenesis. However, it’s important to emphasize that neither of the studies directly reported measurements of myofibril number. Moreover, the two studies provided mixed results with respect to how an increase in mechanical loading impacts myofibril CSA. Hence, to further address this topic, we developed a high-resolution method for visualizing myofibrils with a standard fluorescence microscope and coupled the imaging workflow with pipelines that could perform automated measurements of myofibril size and number (103). We call the method FIM-ID and we used it to examine whether the radial growth of muscle fibers that occurs in response to chronic mechanical overload is mediated by radial growth of the myofibrils and/or myofibrillogenesis. The results of our analyses revealed that 16 days of chronic mechanical overload led to a 28% increase in the CSA of the fibers of the plantaris muscle along with a 29% increase in the total CSA of the myofibrils per fiber. Our analyses also revealed that the CSA of the individual myofibrils increased by 9% whereas the total number of myofibrils per fiber increased by 20%. In the same study, we also subjected a group of men to 7 weeks of resistance exercise, and through the use of FIM-ID we found that it led to a 18% increase in the CSA of the Type II fibers of the vastus lateralis muscles. The increase in muscle fiber CSA was matched by an 18% increase in the total CSA of the myofibrils per fiber, and again, this increase was primarily mediated by an increase in the number of myofibrils per fiber (103).

In summary, our understanding of whether the radial growth of muscle fibers is mediated by radial growth of the myofibrils and/or myofibrillogenesis is in its infancy. Based on the limited studies that have been conducted, it appears that the radial growth of muscle fibers is primarily mediated by myofibrillogenesis. However, it’s very possible that the contribution from radial growth of the myofibrils vs. myofibrillogenesis could vary over time. For instance, Goldspink’s model of myofibril splitting contends that, during the radial growth of muscle fibers, myofibrils initially experience an increase in CSA. Then, as the CSA of the myofibrils increases, the outward radial forces exerted on the Z-discs increase and this can cause the Z-discs to break (75, 76, 78, 139). It is proposed that such breaks can propagate throughout the myofibril and, in turn, result in the formation of two smaller daughter myofibrils. Importantly, the temporal nature of all of the findings from the studies cited in this section are consistent with Goldspink’s model. However, a different form of myofibrillogenesis that involves the de novo formation of myofibrils from nascent proteins has also been described (56, 147, 161, 197). To date, the concepts of de novo myofibrillogenesis have primarily been studied during myogenesis (e.g., during the early stages of differentiation, embryonic development, etc.), and whether this form of myofibrillogenesis could contribute to the radial growth of muscle fibers that occurs in response to an increase in mechanical loading has not been explored. In our opinion, the concepts of myofibril splitting and de novo myofibrillogenesis are equally intriguing and both should be the subject of further investigations.

sec.title: Changes in Myofibril CSA vs. Myofibril Number During Disuse Atrophy
sec.p: In section 5.2 it was concluded that the radial atrophy of muscle fibers that occurs in response to disuse is most likely due to a proportionate decrease in the area that is occupied by the myofibrils and the interstitial elements. Since the majority of the muscle fiber volume is made up of myofibrils, it can be deduced that the radial atrophy of the muscle fibers is largely mediated by either: (i) a decrease in the CSA of the existing myofibrils (i.e., radial atrophy of the myofibrils), and/or (ii) a decrease in the number of myofibrils per muscle fiber (i.e., myofibril hypoplasia). However, despite their fundamental importance, these seemingly simple concepts have not been readily explored. Indeed, we are not aware of any studies that directly examined how the CSA of individual myofibrils, or the number of myofibrils per muscle fiber, is impacted by disuse.

While no studies have directly assessed how disuse affects the CSA of individual myofibrils, two studies have reported measurements of sarcomere diameter from longitudinal sections. Specifically, Giordano et al. (2014) subjected the plantaris muscle of rats to hindlimb suspension for 10 days and found that the average diameter of the sarcomeres decreased by 50% (71). Likewise, Riley et al. (1990) determined that 12.5 days of spaceflight led to a 23% decrease in the diameter of sarcomeres in the adductor longus muscles of rats (149). In addition to these studies, several qualitative investigations have also suggested that disuse leads to a reduction in myofibril diameter (150–153, 192, 203). Moreover, it has been reported that subjecting the soleus muscle of rabbits to 4 weeks of disuse via tenotomy led to a 54% decrease in the number of thin filaments per sarcomere (23). Thus, it seems rather likely that the radial atrophy of muscle fibers that occurs in response to disuse is mediated, at least in part, by radial atrophy of the myofibrils.

Similar to the scarcity of studies that have addressed the effects of disuse on myofibril CSA, we are also not aware of any studies that have examined whether disuse leads to a loss in the number of myofibrils per fiber. However, some insight into this topic can be derived from the work of Riley et al. (1990). Specifically, this study revealed that 12.5 days of space flight led to a 36% decrease in the CSA of the muscle fibers of the adductor longus muscles of rats, and this was associated with a 23% decrease in the mean diameter of the sarcomeres (149). Since sarcomeres/myofibrils are typically cylindrical in shape, a 23% decrease in the sarcomere diameter would equate to a 41% decrease in the myofibril CSA. Based on this derivation, it could be argued that the radial atrophy of the muscle fibers was entirely due to the radial atrophy of the myofibrils. Clearly, however, more studies will be needed before any firm conclusions can be drawn about the role that changes in myofibril CSA vs. myofibril number play in the disuse-induced radial atrophy of muscle fibers.

sec.title: Closing Remarks
sec.p: As mentioned in the introduction, the development of therapies that can sustain, restore, or enhance skeletal muscle mass is of immense clinical and fiscal significance. However, the development of such therapies will require a comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms that regulate skeletal muscle mass. This chapter illustrated that changes in mechanical loading can lead to profound changes in skeletal muscle mass. At the macroscopic and microscopic levels, the structural adaptations that accompany the changes in muscle mass have been fairly well described. However, critical gaps in our knowledge about the ultrastructural adaptations remain. This lack of information is particularly important because the ultrastructural adaptations will effectively serve as the foundational events that drive the mechanical load-induced changes in muscle mass. Fortunately, with the emergence of methods like FIM-ID, these ultrastructural adaptations can now be studied in a time- and cost-effective manner. It is our hope that the use of such methods will lead to rapid advancements in the field and, in turn, create a framework of knowledge for future studies that are aimed at developing a better understanding of the mechanisms that control the ultrastructural adaptations, if / why these mechanisms fail in various disease states, and whether they can be manipulated to improve therapies that are aimed at increasing skeletal muscle mass and function. Simply put, the prospects for this area of research are bright, but we will have to look closely if we want to see it.

caption.p: (A) Illustration of skeletal muscle structure copied with permission under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/deed.en) and adapted for this chapter (99). (B) Cross-section of a mouse plantaris muscle that was subjected to immunohistochemistry for the identification of Type IIA (cyan), and Type IIB (magenta) muscle fibers as well as laminin to identify the endomysium (white). (C) Cross-section of a mouse plantaris muscle that was subjected to immunohistochemistry for the identification of dystrophin to identify the inner boundary of the sarcolemma (white) and nuclei (green). (D) Cross-section of a mouse plantaris muscle that was subjected to electron microscopy to highlight the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) that surrounds individual myofibrils as well as the mitochondria (Mito) that run between the myofibrils. (E) Higher magnification of the boxed region in D reveals the presence of the thick and thin myofilaments. All panels in this figure were originally presented by Jorgenson et al., 2020 (102).

Various pennation angle arrangements of the fascicles / muscle fibers in skeletal muscle: (A) fusiform, (B) unipennate, (C) bipennante, (D) multipennate. Blue lines indicate the plane for the anatomical cross-sectional area (CSA) (i.e., the CSA that runs perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the muscle), and green lines indicate the plane for physiological CSA (i.e., the CSA that runs perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the fascicles / muscle fibers). Adapted under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported license from original work by Uwe Gille (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/deed.en).

Illustration of how radial and longitudinal growth/atrophy of the fascicles could induce changes at the whole muscle level. (A) A hypothetical muscle and its basic architectural properties. The geometric model of Maxwell et al., (1974) (119) was used to predict how a 25% decrease in fascicle diameter (B), or a 25% increase in fascicle length (C), would impact measurements of whole muscle length and whole muscle diameter. (D) Illustrates how the same increase in whole muscle diameter observed in A vs. C could be achieved by an increase in the diameter and pennation angle of the fascicles.
